draft.introduction: Bede’s Ecclesiastical History of the English People (731 CE) narrates the conversion of the Anglo-Saxons and the establishment of Christianity in Britain. It is the foundational work of English historiography.

translation.text: Britain, an island in the ocean, formerly called Albion, lies to the northwest, facing the coasts of Germany, France, and Spain. It extends eight hundred miles to the north and is two hundred miles broad. It has twenty-eight noble cities and is remarkable for its rivers and springs. The island was first inhabited by the Britons, who came from Armorica.
translation.original_text: Britannia Oceani insula, cui quondam Albion nomen fuit

translator_notes.note: Bede’s careful citation of sources, chronological precision, and critical evaluation of testimony make this the first work of English history written according to scholarly method. His adoption of the anno Domini dating system popularized it throughout Christendom.
